questions.question: At 10 Mbps, the slot time is 512 bit-times. Which value correctly converts this to microseconds?
options: 512 µs
questions.explanation: One bit at 10 Mbps takes 100 ns, so 512 bit-times = 512 × 100 ns = 51,200 ns = 51.2 µs. This slot time supports a maximum cable path of ~2,500 m.

questions.question: In CSMA/CD, why must the minimum Ethernet frame be at least 64 bytes?
options: To match the maximum size of a network-layer IP packet header
So that the frame transmission time is at least one slot time, guaranteeing the sender is still transmitting when a collision echo returns
To ensure the CRC-32 FCS field can detect all single-bit errors across the frame
To prevent fragmentation by routers when the frame crosses a WAN link
questions.explanation: A sender must still be transmitting when a collision signal returns from the far end of the cable. The 64-byte minimum makes the transmission last at least one slot time (2 × one-way propagation delay), so any collision is detected before the sender releases the medium.

questions.question: What byte value does a Gigabit Ethernet NIC use as the carrier extension fill symbol, and why can it not be confused with frame data?
options: 0x00, because null bytes are never valid inside an Ethernet payload
0x0F, because it is a reserved 8B/10B K-code control character that cannot appear in data codewords
0xFF, because all-ones patterns are filtered out by the FCS check
0xAA, because it matches the preamble and keeps the receiver's clock locked
questions.explanation: 0x0F is a reserved 8B/10B K-code (control character). In 8B/10B encoding, K-codes occupy a separate code-space from D-codes (data), so a receiver always knows that 0x0F fill bytes are extension symbols, not payload. The receiving NIC strips them before delivering the frame to the OS.

questions.question: A Gigabit Ethernet NIC transmits the smallest legal frame (46-byte payload, 64-byte MAC frame) with carrier extension. What is the payload efficiency on the wire?
questions.explanation: Carrier extension pads the 64-byte frame to 512 bytes on the wire. Payload efficiency = 46 / 512 ≈ 9%. A 1-Gbps link carrying only minimum-size frames therefore delivers roughly 90 Mbps of user data. Frame bursting recovers much of this loss for queued senders.

questions.question: A sender has 20 queued 64-byte frames and uses frame bursting. What is the total on-wire byte count for the burst, and why is it structured that way?
options: 1280 bytes — all 20 frames are transmitted back-to-back with no fill
1956 bytes — frame 1 is carrier-extended to 512 bytes; frames 2–20 are each preceded by 12 bytes of inter-frame extension fill
10240 bytes — each frame is carrier-extended individually to 512 bytes
65536 bytes — the burst always occupies the full burst-cap window
questions.explanation: Frame 1 uses carrier extension: 512 bytes. Frames 2–20 (19 frames) each add 64 bytes of frame data plus 12 bytes of IFE fill (0x0F symbols) to keep the medium marked busy. Total = 512 + 19 × (64 + 12) = 512 + 1444 = 1956 bytes. Payload efficiency ≈ 920 / 1956 ≈ 47%, versus 9% with individual carrier-extended frames.

questions.question: Why does 1000Base-SX (fiber Gigabit Ethernet) require a 1.25 Gbaud signaling rate to deliver 1 Gbps of user data?
options: PAM-5 signaling encodes 2 bits per symbol, so twice as many symbols are needed
8B/10B encoding maps every 8 data bits to a 10-bit codeword, adding 25% overhead and requiring 1 Gbps × 10/8 = 1.25 Gbaud
64B/66B encoding adds 2 overhead bits per 64-bit block, requiring a 3.1% higher clock
Manchester encoding doubles the symbol rate to 2 Gbaud for guaranteed transitions
questions.explanation: 8B/10B (borrowed from Fibre Channel) maps each 8-bit data byte to a 10-bit codeword chosen to maintain DC balance via running disparity. This 25% coding overhead means the physical layer must clock at 1 Gbps × (10/8) = 1.25 Gbaud. 1000Base-T uses a completely different approach (PAM-5 on four pairs with DSP echo cancellation) precisely to avoid this fiber-specific constraint.
